Basin Average Precipitation-Frequency Using Max Stable Processes Chapter 1 Introduction & Overview

Basin Average Precipitation-Frequency Using Max Stable Processes Andrew Verdin 2026-06-04 Chapter 1 Introduction & Overview This document presents a cohesive R package (RMCmsp) created with the purpose of streamlining the computation of basin-average precipitation-frequency curves using Max-Stable Processes (MSP). An example workflow is included in the R package as a tutorial. The functions contained within RMCmsp are essentially the compilation and optimization of R scripts originally written for the basin-average precipitation-frequency analysis for the Painted Rock Dam watershed. These original R scripts were created by Brian Skahill of the USACE Engineer Research and Development Center (ERDC), and were recently organized and streamlined by Andrew Verdin and Samantha Hartke of the USACE Risk Management Center (RMC). This document presents an overview of the basic functions of the MSP spatial extremes workflow. There are endless bells and whistles that can/will eventually be integrated into this R package, as time permits. The basic functions can be summarized as follows: Use the msp_setup( ) function to set up the MSP workspace Estimate the GEV parameters at each site independently Extract PRISM covariates for each site Identify optimal PRISM covariates to generate GEV trend rasters Optionally, use the plot_at_site_gev( ) function to visualize the spatial distribution of GEV parameters (Note, the shapefile will not plot if you are on the network, because efficiency!) Use the fit_msp( ) function to fit the general MSP model (marginals and spatial covariance, simultaneously) Fit spatial model for GEV parameters (i.e., marginals) Fit initial MSP for spatial covariance parameters Fit general MSP model (i.e., marginals and covariance simultaneously) Use the msp_uncertainty( ) function to create an ensemble of general MSP model parameters. You can choose to do this using random samples from a multivariate normal distribution (method = "mvn"), and this will take < 1 second You can also choose the brute force way (method = "boot"), but this will take 24+ hours…). Please, just don’t do this right now c.nsims are the number of parameter sets you want to create. This will bottleneck significantly with method = boot Note that you can generate more samples very quickly with method = mvn, but the bottleneck comes when passing those samples to simulate_from_msp( ) in the next step. Start with something like nsims = 20 for testing. For a final estimate, nsims = 300 is usually good Use the simulate_from_msp( ) function to create the basin-average precipitation-frequency curves using the general MSP model with MSP uncertainty This function takes the output from the msp_uncertainty( ) function, the output from the fit_msp( ) function, the number of simulations desired, and the target locations and weights as input The number of simulations modulates how rare of a precipitation-frequency estimate you want (e.g., nsims=10000 results in estimates out to 1E-4) The target locations and weights should be significantly less than the number of grid cells in the watershed. Think of these as subbasin centroids, where the weights are the subbasin areas Optionally, you can use the msp_smooth( ) function to produce parametric representation of the nonparametric precipitation-frequency curves This function takes the output from simulate_from_msp( ) function and gives you a lot of cool information gev.params - five columns: gev.loc, gev.scale, gev.shape, optim, and convergence. The number of rows is equal to the number of simulations from msp_uncertainty( ) gev.quants - number of columns equal to the number of simulations from msp_uncertainty( ) plus 1. The extra column holds the AEP for these quantiles. Each column holds quantiles for the corresponding GEV parameters from gev.params gev.table - a table with AEP, Upper, Lower, and Mean precipitation-frequency estimates (Upper and Lower correspond to the confidence interval specified by the user, e.g., ci=90 means a 90% confidence interval) ci - the specified confidence interval in percent (e.g., ci=90) mean.gev.params - the GEV distribution parameters that best represent the mean precipitation-frequency curve gev.erl - the effective record length to quantify uncertainty around mean.gev.params Currently, the simulate_from_msp( ) function requires output from the msp_uncertainty( ) function. The functionality to simulate from a single general MSP model (i.e., output from the fit_msp( ) function) will be incorporated as time permits. However, given the pretty quick approach to uncertainty (i.e., method = mvn), I recommend always running with uncertainty (e.g., nsims = 20 in msp_uncertainty( )). Additionally, note that the gev.erl is NOT based on the number of simulations, but rather is a function of the input data. You can run 2 uncertainty simulations (e.g., nsims=2 in msp_uncertainty( )) and end up with the exact same ERL you get when you run nsims=500. However, the mean.gev.params are a function of the number of simulations and need to be estimated robustly (e.g., nsims=100 minimum). You shouldn’t need the mean GEV parameters and ERL for most applications, but we include them for convenience. Putting the mean.gev.params and gev.erl into software like TotalRisk will get you a GEV curve with uncertainty very quickly and efficiently :) Before any of these steps can be taken, however, the details on data preparation are discussed. In addition to the data preparation, quality control is crucial (see Appendix A for a thorough quality control protocol). Following the data preparation discussion, each of the steps listed above will be discussed in detail in its own section. An example workflow and a list of required packages are provided in Appendix B. If you have your data ready to go, skip to Chapter 3 bookdown::serve_book()

Chapter 2 Preparing the Precipitation Database 2.1 Download GHCNd stations 2.2 Combine and Organize GHCNd Data 2.3 Calculate Annual Maxima 2.4 PRISM Variables 2.5 Targets and Target Areas 2.6 Setting Up Your Workspace

Chapter 2 Preparing the Precipitation Database The example workflow presented in this document relies on daily precipitation observations from the Global Historical Climatology Network (GHCN; Menne et al. 2012). Download the station inventory for the GHCN-daily dataset (GHCNd). Pull the data into a spreadsheet and save as a csv named ghcnd-inventory.csv. Give the columns the following names: ghcnd_id, lat, lon, varid, startyr, endyr. The startyr and endyr columns aren’t used in this analysis. Note that you need to define two sub-directories within the MSP analysis directory named Data and input_files. Data will hold the GHCNd station data you are going to download; input_files will save the resulting annual maxima and metadata, along with all other required inputs for the MSP analysis. df <- read.csv("ghcnd-inventory.csv") head(df) ## ghcn_id lat lon varid startyr endyr ## 1 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 TMAX 1949 1949 ## 2 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 TMIN 1949 1949 ## 3 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 PRCP 1949 1949 ## 4 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 SNOW 1949 1949 ## 5 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 SNWD 1949 1949 ## 6 ACW00011604 17.1167 -61.7833 PGTM 1949 1949 2.1 Download GHCNd stations Plot the stations in GIS with your basin shapefile and pertinent raster data to assist in the definition of a study region surrounding the basin. Consult a hydrometeorologist (such as John England, Samantha Hartke, or Andrew Verdin) if you need help defining a bounding box. Once you have the bounding box defined, create a subset of the station inventory and download the data for those stations using the following R code (0a_download_ghcnd.R): library(curl) # set your working directory to the folder you want to save all the GHCNd data setwd("C:/Path/To/MSP/Analysis/Data/") # define the output directory - this will be the input to the MSP, call it "input_data" idir = "C:/Path/To/MSP/Analysis/input_data/" # read in the inventory file, do NOT put the inventory file in your data folder. df.m = read.csv("C:/Path/To/MSP/Analysis/ghcnd-inventory.csv") # take subset of inventory file; constrain to bounding box and only PRCP stations # change your bounding box as required! df.sub = subset(df.m, lon > -85 & lon < -82.8 & lat > 33.5 & lat < 35.5) df.sub = subset(df.sub, varid == "PRCP") # set up a progress bar to gauge how long it will take pb = txtProgressBar(min=0,max=nrow(df.sub),style=3) # loop through each row of the subset and attempts to download a file for that station. # destfile is saving it to your current working directory, use getwd() to check where for(i in 1:nrow(df.sub)){ setTxtProgressBar(pb,i) curl_download(url=paste0("https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/",df.sub$ghcnd_id[i],".csv"), destfile=paste0(df.sub$ghcnd_id[i],".csv")) } # save the subset file for later use. do NOT save the subset file to the data folder write.csv(df.sub, paste0(idir, "GHCNd-metadata.csv"), row.names=FALSE) 2.2 Combine and Organize GHCNd Data After downloading all the data for the stations in the bounding box, that data needs to be extracted and put into a (more) concise format. The first step is to loop through all the files to define the earliest and latest date to set up a matrix that will hold all sites (rows) and observations (columns) for every day from the earliest to the latest date. This will be a huge matrix! Use the following R code for this task (0b_ghcnd_dataset.R): library(lubridate) library(plyr) # read in that subset we made from the station inventory and bounding box df.meta = read.csv(paste0(idir,"GHCNd-metadata.csv")) # fn is a list of all filenames; fns is the same with “.csv” removed (just site ID) fn = list.files(pattern=".csv") fns = unlist(strsplit(fn, split=".csv")) # start with some arbitrary min and max dates – update as needed for your analysis mind = ymd("2005-01-01") maxd = ymd("2005-12-31") # loop through and update ‘mind’ and ‘maxd’ variables as needed pb = txtProgressBar(min=0, max=length(fn), style=3) for(i in 1:length(fn)){ setTxtProgressBar(pb,i) df = read.csv(fn[i]) if(min(ymd(df$DATE)) < mind) mind = min(ymd(df$DATE)) if(max(ymd(df$DATE)) > maxd) maxd = max(ymd(df$DATE)) } # define vector of all dates, and the matrix that will hold all the data full_dates = data.frame(DATE=seq.Date(from=ymd(mind), to=ymd(maxd), by="day")) df.daily = matrix(NA, nrow=nrow(full_dates), ncol=length(fn)) # loop through and populate that matrix pb = txtProgressBar(min=0,max=ncol(df.daily),style=3) for(i in 1:ncol(df.daily)){ setTxtProgressBar(pb,i) tmp = read.csv(fn[i]) sub = subset(tmp, select=c("DATE","PRCP")) sub$DATE = ymd(sub$DATE) tmp.full = join(full_dates, sub, by="DATE") df.daily[,i] = tmp.full$PRCP } # put the station id in the column name colnames(df.daily) = fns # save it. do NOT save the data matrix to the data folder write.csv(df.daily, paste0(idir,"ghcn-daily.csv"), row.names=FALSE) 2.3 Calculate Annual Maxima Now that the daily precipitation observations have been put into the big data matrix, it’s time to process those annual maxima. For this approach we are looking at the calendar year annual maximum n-day precipitation observation at each site independently. Use the following R code to create the n-day annual maxima (0c_calc_anmax.R): library(zoo) library(plyr) # define output matrix (nrow = years, ncol = stations) anmax.out = matrix(NA, nrow=length(unique(year(ymd(full_dates$DATE)))), ncol=length(fn)) # 2-day critical duration (currently only works for daily scale, we're using GHCNd after all!) dur = 2 # loop through each row of df.daily and calculate annual maxima pb = txtProgressBar(min=0, max=ncol(df.daily), style=3) for(i in 1:ncol(df.daily)){ setTxtProgressBar(pb,i) tmp.stn = data.frame(DATE=full_dates$DATE, prcp=as.numeric(df.daily[,i])) tmp.stn$yr = year(ymd(tmp.stn$DATE)) tmp.stn$nday = rollsum(tmp.stn$prcp, k=dur, align="right", na.pad=TRUE) tmp.out = ddply(tmp.stn, .(yr), summarise, maxp = max(nday,na.rm=TRUE)) tmp.out$maxp[tmp.out$maxp < 0] = NA anmax.out[,i] = as.numeric(tmp.out$maxp) } You should only save those sites that have at least ~20 years of data, as follows: # boolean indicating which sites have at least 20 observations min.id = apply(!is.na(anmax.out), 2, sum) > 19 anmax.out2 = anmax.out[,min.id] df.meta.out = df.meta[min.id,] # save it. do NOT save the annual maxima to the data folder write.csv(anmax.out2, paste0(idir,"atsite-anmax-",dur,"-day.csv"), row.names=FALSE) write.csv(df.meta.out, paste0(idir,"atsite-anmax-",dur,"-day-metadata.csv"), row.names=FALSE) 2.4 PRISM Variables PRISM variables are used to create the trend rasters, which are essentially interpolated GEV parameters. PRISM variables come in 800m and 4km resolution for CONUS, so you want to crop those rasters to the general study area to make them easier to work with. I would recommend using the 4km to avoid blowing up your laptop. First, download all the PRISM variables from the website. To do this, navigate to the 30-Year Normals page. Make sure the 4km spatial resolution radio button is selected. You want to download every variable for the annual temporal period. Make sure the annual data radio button is selected. Then, one by one, select each variable from the drop down menu and download the data in .bil format. Alternatively, you can click Download All Normals Data (.bil). This will save you clicking time but will take up more room on your hard drive. knitr::include_url("https://prism.oregonstate.edu/normals/") I just went back to the PRISM website while working through this document, and there is no more Download All Normals Data (.bil) option. Also, the solar irradiance variables are unavailable. You can download all the PRISM data you need for this analysis here: \\CPC-Netapp3.eis.ds.usace.army.mil\RMC\RMC-HECSA\TRANSFER\RMC\Verdin\RMCmsp_v1.0_beta\PRISM_MSP Once all data have been downloaded, you can use the following code (0d_crop_prism.R) to crop them to a user-defined bounding box and save them all in an RDS file. library(terra) # set datadir to directory where PRISM was downloaded datadir = "C:/Path/To/PRISM/Data/" # load the metadata from the previous step meta = read.csv(paste0(idir,"/atsite-anmax-",dur,"-day-metadata.csv")) # these are the raster variables we're going to use - DO NOT MODIFY THESE TWO LINES raster_vars = c("ppt", "tmean", "tmax", "tmin", "tdmean", "vpdmax", "vpdmin", "solclear", "solslope", "soltotal", "soltrans") m_vars = c(4, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 3, 3, 3, 3) # read in the first prism file prism_file = paste0(datadir,"/PRISM_VARIABLE_30yr_normal_800mM5_annual_asc.asc") # Define the bounding box with extra buffer to avoid NA at stations lon_min = floor(min(meta$lon)) lon_max = ceiling(max(meta$lon)) lat_min = floor(min(meta$lat)) lat_max = ceiling(max(meta$lat)) # Define the extent of the bounding box bbox_extent = ext(lon_min, lon_max, lat_min, lat_max) # loop through all raster variables, modify the string/filename, read and crop # assign puts the cropped prism data into that variable name for convenience :) pb = txtProgressBar(min=0, max=length(raster_vars), style=3) for(i in 1:length(raster_vars)){ setTxtProgressBar(pb,i) var = raster_vars[i] modified_string <- gsub("VARIABLE", var, prism_file) PRISM_data = rast(gsub('5', m_vars[i], modified_string)) PRISM_data <- crop(PRISM_data, bbox_extent) assign(var, PRISM_data) } # read in the DEM and crop PRISM_us_dem_800m_r = rast(paste0(datadir,"/PRISM_us_dem_800m_bil.bil")) PRISM_us_dem_800m_r = crop(PRISM_us_dem_800m_r, bbox_extent) # put them all in a list and save as RDS file list.out <- list(Z = PRISM_us_dem_800m_r, ppt = ppt, tmean = tmean, tmax = tmax, tmin = tmin, tdmean = tdmean, vpdmax = vpdmax, vpdmin = vpdmin, solclear = solclear, solslope = solslope, soltotal = soltotal, soltrans = soltrans) saveRDS(list.out, paste0(idir,"/prism_crop.rds")) 2.5 Targets and Target Areas MSP simulations are very computationally expensive. Sensitivities were run by Brian Skahill on the Trinity and Willamette River basins (Skahill et al. 2023) and it was determined that defining some target locations and assigning areas to those locations using Thiessen polygons created basin average precipitation-frequency curves comparable to those when simulating at all pixels. How you define those target locations and areas should be a project specific task, with good representation of orographic influence, topography, and hydroclimatology. The required input for the MSP workflow is a matrix with latitude, longitude, and area for the target locations. To be compatible with these functions, the column names need to be: lat, lon, and area. The targets are occasionally referred to as subbasin targets because this is one method that could be used. If there is an existing HMS model, the subbasin shapefile can be used to define the target locations as the subbasin centroids, with the area being the total area of each subbasin. Another option could be equally spaced points across a watershed. The tricky part here is obtaining the corresponding area. For this, a routine such as Thiessen polygons comes in handy. 2.6 Setting Up Your Workspace You now have all the required inputs to get started on your MSP analysis! The next chapter will walk you through the four main functions included in the RMCmsp library. See Appendix B for an example workflow.
Chapter 3 Workflow for MSP Precipitation-Frequency 3.1 Step 1: msp_setup( ) 3.2 Step 2: fit_msp( ) 3.3 Step 3: msp_uncertainty( ) 3.4 Step 4: simulate_from_msp( ) 3.5 Step 5: Bonus Functions

Chapter 3 Workflow for MSP Precipitation-Frequency The following subsections describe the four-step workflow used to develop basin-average precipitation-frequency estimates with uncertainty using the RMCmsp library. 3.1 Step 1: msp_setup( ) As mentioned in the Chapter 1, the first function was created to set up the MSP workspace. Required inputs for the msp_setup( ) function include: anmax: Matrix of annual maximum precipitation. Rows are years and columns are stations. Missing values represented with NA. Extend the record back to the first observation at any site. metadata: Matrix of metadata corresponding to the annual maximum precipitation data set. Rows are stations and columns are the geostationary information. Required columns are: "lon", "lat", "stnid". prism_rasters: An RDS file containing the cropped PRISM rasters, to be used as covariates and for trend raster development, as in Section 2.4. The RDS file contains the PRISM rasters of class list. subbasin_targets: A matrix of coordinates in decimal degrees (longlat) defining the target locations for MSP simulation. The target locations can be subbasin centroids for very large watersheds or regularly-spaced coordinates across a smaller watershed. The formatting requirements are described in Section 2.5. Optionally, alpha and nfolds, two parameters for the elastic net parameter optimization routine. Defaults are alpha=0.95 and nfolds=175. If you don’t know what this means, don’t change them! Now that the required inputs are sorted out, it’s time to get started on the four-step workflow. Step one is using the msp_setup( ) function to: Fit GEV distributions at each station independently, Extract PRISM covariates for each station, Optimize the PRISM covariates used as predictors in the spatial models for GEV location, scale, and shape, independently. Optionally, using the plot_at_site_gev( ) function to visualize the spatial distribution of GEV parameters. This is useful for troubleshooting any sites that may have wildly inconsistent parameters. Based on Figure 3.1, do you see any sites that might be worth investigating? (Note the watershed is very small in this example - you can see it in the center of the GEV plots). library(devtools) load_all() msp_workspace <- msp_setup(anmax=mc_anmax, metadata=mc_meta, prism_rasters=mc_prism, subbasin_targets=mc_targets) plot_at_site_gev(at_site_gev = msp_workspace$gev.fits, shp=mc_shapefile) Figure 3.1: Plot of GEV location (left), scale (middle), and shape (right) for all sites in MSP analysis. Note that the input data are baked into the R package, so when you install the package you get the data. It’s lazy loaded data, which means it doesn’t show up in your environment until you use it. The msp_setup( ) function returns a list with 12 items: anmax: the annual maximum series for your project gev.fits: the fitted GEV distribution parameters for each site covar_normalized: a matrix of PRISM variables, normalized so each has mean and standard deviation of zero and one, respectively covar_colmean: a vector of column means used to normalize the PRISM variables, used for transforming the full PRISM grids covar_colstd: a vector of column standard deviations used to normalize the PRISM variables, used for transforming the full PRISM grids targets_normalized: normalized coordinates for subbasin target locations glmnet_cvfit_loc: model coefficients for the optimized GEV location model glmnet_cvfit_scale: model coefficients for the optimized GEV scale model glmnet_cvfit_shape: model coefficients for the optimized GEV shape model prism_rasters: cropped PRISM rasters used to develop the the trend rasters for location, scale, and shape targets_prism: PRISM values extracted to subbasin target locations metadata: the station metadata for your project 3.2 Step 2: fit_msp( ) Your workspace is now set up with everything you need to fit the MSP model! The fit_msp( ) function takes three input parameters, but only one is really needed to get started: msp_workspace: The result of the msp_setup( ) function. All the information you need to fit the MSP model is included in this one variable! Optionally, covar.mod: This input variable defaults to ‘twhitmat’, which represents the Extremal-t model with a Whittle-Matern correlation function. If this doesn’t make sense to you, don’t change this input variable! Valid models for this variable are: "tpowexp", "tcauchy", "twhitmat", "tbessel", "tcaugen" Optionally, parscale.min: This input variable represents the minimum absolute value used when constructing the parscale vector for the joint model optimizer. Prevents near-zero scales from destabilizing numerical optimization. Default is 0.01. Again, don’t change it if this makes no sense to you. You should be able to take your MSP workspace (defined as msp_workspace in Step 1) and put it into the fit_msp( ) function with success. There may be some issues if the data are not properly QC’d, the domain is not properly defined, and other issues that come from noisy spatial data. If you’re getting failed joint model fits from fit_msp( ) and you’re out of ideas, bring your issues to those hydrometeorologists identified in Section 2.1. msp_fit <- fit_msp(msp_workspace=msp_workspace, covar.mod='twhitmat') Some messages will print to your R console while this model runs. Depending on the size and complexity of your domain, this could take a while (I have personally seen a joint model take 30 minutes to fit). The messages help you understand the three steps that are containd within the fit_msp( ) function: "Fitting spatial GEV model...": Fit the spatial model for GEV parameters (i.e., marginals) "Fitting initial MSP..." "User-defined covariance model: twhitmat": Fit the initial MSP for spatial covariance parameters "Fitting general MSP model...": Fit the joint model (i.e., the general MSP), which estimates the marginals and covariance simultaneously. The general MSP model is what takes the longest. Hopefully once it stops running you see the following message: "Joint MSP model fitted successfully.". If it fails to converge on a fitted model, you will see a detailed error message describing what went wrong. Consult those hydrometeorologists :) Assuming your general MSP model fitted successfully, the fit_msp( ) function returns a list with 8 items: spatial_gev: the fitted spatial GEV model, output from the fitspatgev( ) function, used as initial estimates for the general MSP model initial_msp: the fitted initial MSP model, output from the fitmaxstab( ) function, used as initial estimates for the general MSP model general_msp: the fitted general MSP model, if the general MSP model converged successfully. NULL if the general MSP model did not converge successfully general_msp_status: the status of the general MSP model, one of: "converged", "fit_error", "matrix_error". "converged" indicates the general MSP model was successfully fitted; "fit_error" indicates the general MSP model did not converge; "matrix_error" indicates the model converged but produced an invalid hessian and/or variance covariance matrix msp_workspace: the MSP workspace that was provided to the fit_msp( ) function, which is the output from msp_setup( ) loc.form: optimized equation for GEV location trend raster development, output from the glmnet optimization scale.form: optimized equation for GEV scale trend raster development, output from the glmnet optimization shape.form: optimized equation for GEV shape trend raster development, output from the glmnet optimization 3.3 Step 3: msp_uncertainty( ) Assuming the general MSP model converged and fitted successfully, you can now take the output from fit_msp( ) and put it into the msp_uncertainty( ) function. msp_uncert_mvn <- msp_uncertainty(msp_fit=msp_fit, method="mvn", nsims=100) As mentioned in the introduction, there are technically two methods to choose from when running the msp_uncertainty( ) function. Based on testing at two very different watersheds, you should choose method="mvn". This uses the general MSP model fitted values and variance covariance matrix to produce random samples from a multivariate normal (i.e., mvn) distribution. The other method (method="boot") will take random samples from the general MSP model and re-fit an MSP model to each. Remember how long it took to fit the general MSP model? Multiply that by 300. Or maybe only 50 because it’s parallelized. Either way, it takes forever. Only do it if you like waiting. Here are validation figures from those two watersheds: Figure 3.2: Output from fit_spatial_GLM function, describing the spatial GEV model. The msp_uncertainty( ) function returns a list with four items: param_samples: matrix of the general MSP model parameters. The number of rows corresponds to nsims specified in the msp_uncertainty( ) function call; the number of columns represents the number of parameters in the general MSP model uncertainty_method: the method used to generate general MSP model parameters. Options are mvn or boot general_msp_used: boolean indicating whether the general MSP model was used. This should always be TRUE msp_fit: the fitted general MSP model and accompanying information provided by the fit_msp( ) function, provided as input to msp_uncertainty( ) 3.4 Step 4: simulate_from_msp( ) Now that we have the matrix of general MSP model parameters to quantify our uncertainty, we need to create the precipitation-frequency curves for each of the simulations. In this step, we take the bootstrapped MSP model parameters and generate thousands of random samples of annual maxima to produce the nonparametric basin-average precipitation-frequency curves with uncertainty! The annual maxima samples are generated at the target locations, and the basin averages are calculated using the target location weights. The number of random samples determines how rare of a precipitation-frequency estimate you will generate. In the example below, we specify nsims=10000, which results in precipitation-frequency estimates as rare as 1e-4 (i.e., 1/10,000). msp_sims_mvn <- simulate_from_msp(msp_uncertainty=msp_uncert_mvn, msp_fit=msp_fit, nsims=10000, subbasin_targets=mc_targets, target_weights=mc_weights) Under the hood, we are building trend rasters for each parameter set from the msp_uncertainty( ) output. The trend rasters are essentially interpolated GEV location, scale, and shape parameters. These GEV parameters are used to transform our simulations from the native Frechet space back into GEV space. For larger watersheds with a lot of subbasin targets, this step may take a while. The process is parallelized, though, so it’s been pretty quick for my test basins. The simulate_from_msp( ) function returns a list with 10 items: pf_curve: the nonparametric precipitation-frequency curves with uncertainty. Number of rows equals number of simulations in the simulate_from_msp( ) function (e.g., nsims=10000 in the simulate_from_msp( ) function); number of columns is six: "pp", "aep", "lower", "mean", "model", "upper". "pp" is the plotting position, "aep" is the corresponding annual exceedance probability, "lower" is the 5th percentile across all models (i.e., lower bound of 90% confidence interval), "mean" is the mean across all models, "model" is the precipitation-frequency curve from the original general MSP model, and "upper" is the 95th percentile across all models (i.e., upper bound of 90% confidence interval) sims_frech: the raw random samples of annual maxima in Frechet space. An object of class list with length equal to the number of uncertainty samples (e.g., nsims=100 in the msp_uncertainty( ) function). Each list object is a matrix with number of rows equal to nsims from the simulate_from_msp( ) function, and number of columns equal to the number of subbasin targets sims_gev: the same simulations as sims_frech only converted back to GEV space mean_gev: the raw basin average precipitation-frequency curves for each uncertainty sample target_locations: GEV location parameters for each uncertainty sample and each target location (rows are samples, columns are subbasin targets) target_scales: GEV scale parameters for each uncertainty sample and each target location (rows are samples, columns are subbasin targets) target_shapes: GEV shape parameters for each uncertainty sample and each target location (rows are samples, columns are subbasin targets) general_msp_used: boolean indicating whether the general MSP model was used. This should always be TRUE msp_uncertainty: the output from the msp_uncertainty( ) function that was used as input for simulate_from_msp( ) msp_fit: the output from the fit_msp( ) function that was used as input for simulate_from_msp( ) 3.5 Step 5: Bonus Functions 3.5.1 msp_smooth( ) 3.5.2 msp_point_precip( )
A Quality Control for Precipitation Annual Maximum Series A.1 Ratio Test A.2 Outlier Test A.3 Extreme GEV Parameters

A Quality Control for Precipitation Annual Maximum Series This appendix provides a suite of quality control tests to be employed to ensure the precipitation dataset does not contain erroneously large observations. Note that if an observation is flagged it is not always recommended to automatically remove that observation. Rather, a closer inspection is warranted. Are nearby stations reporting a similarly large value? Is there an historic storm that occurred on that date in the region? Build the case for why the observation needs to be thrown out. Don’t build the case on why the observation needs to remain in the database. Innocent until proven guilty! A.1 Ratio Test The ratio test is a simple heuristic for identifying observations that may have been recorded erroneously. The ratio of the largest and second-largest observations in the AMS is computed, and any observations exceeding a ratio of 2 are flagged for manual inspection. Flagged observations are then corroborated against event dates and magnitudes at surrounding stations. In data sparse regions a gridded dataset may be suitable for verification. The function qc_ratio_test( ) provides a simple summary of flagged observations for each station. This function is flexible enough to allow the user to specify how many observations to compare. For example, the first and second observations may not be significantly different from each other, but the second and third observations may be where the anomaly occurs. The function qc_ratio_test( ) takes the following input: data: the matrix of annual maxima to be QC’d. Rows are years, columns are stations, station ID should be the column names to enable identification of outliers n_compare: the number of observations to compare. For example, n_compare = 3 computes ratios p1/p2 and p2/p3 threshold: the ratio value above which a pair is flagged. Defaults to 2 verbose: logical. If TRUE, prints a summary to the console. Defaults to TRUE anmax_ratios <- qc_ratio_test(data=mc_anmax, n_compare=3, threshold=2, verbose=TRUE) A.2 Outlier Test The outlier test was implemented as a simplified outlier detection test compared to tests such as the Grubbs-Beck or Multiple Grubbs-Beck tests. Observations falling below 2 standard deviations from the median of the AMS may be removed as a low outlier. Visual inspection of the AMS plotting positions can be further evidence to warrant removal. Observations falling above 2 standard deviations from the median are flagged as high outliers and should be investigated in the same way as observations flagged in the ratio test. The function qc_outlier_test( ) provides a simple summary of flagged observations, and specifies whether they are low or high outliers, for each station. This function computes the at-site median and standard deviation, and flags observations that fall outside the user-specified number of standard deviations from the median. Another option in the function is the ability to log-transform the data before identifying outliers. The function qc_outlier_test( ) takes the following input: data: the matrix of annual maxima to be QC’d. Rows are years, columns are stations, and station ID should be the column names to enable identification of outliers k: the number of standard deviations from the median used as the outlier threshold. Default is 2 log_transform: logical. If TRUE, the test is performed on log(data) and boundard are back-transformed for reporting. Defaults to TRUE verbose: logical. If TRUE, prints a summary to the console. Defaults to TRUE anmax_outliers <- qc_outlier_test(data=mc_anmax, k=2, log_transform=TRUE, verbose=TRUE) A.3 Extreme GEV Parameters As a qualitative quality control step, the site-specific GEV parameters can be plotted using the plot_at_site_gev( ) function to identify sites with anomalous values (See Chapter 3.1). Special attention should be given to the shape parameter, as extreme positive or negative values can produce very steep or flat frequency curves that may be the result of one or more outliers. Sites flagged as having extreme GEV parameters should be reviewed for outliers to determine whether the parameters reflect genuine hydrologic behavior or are artifacts of a short and/or sparse period of record.
B Example Workflow B.1 Using the Data from RMCmsp B.2 Using Your Own Data from Section 2

B Example Workflow B.1 Using the Data from RMCmsp This appendix presents an example workflow using the data that comes baked into the R package. To begin, double click the RStudio Project File in the RMCmsp folder: RMCmsp.Rproj. This opens up an instance of RStudio. You should see “RMCmsp - RStudio” in the top left banner of RStudio. You will need to have devtools installed. From RStudio, type install.packages("devtools") into the Console. Once that is successfully installed, you should be able to run the following workflow: library(devtools) load_all() # Step 1 msp_workspace <- msp_setup(anmax=mc_anmax, metadata=mc_meta, prism_rasters=mc_prism, subbasin_targets=mc_targets) plot_at_site_gev(at_site_gev = msp_workspace$gev.fits, shp=mc_shapefile) # Step 2 msp_fit <- fit_msp(msp_workspace=msp_workspace, covar.mod='twhitmat') # Step 3 msp_uncert_mvn <- msp_uncertainty(msp_fit=msp_fit, method="mvn", nsims=100) # Step 4 msp_sims_mvn <- simulate_from_msp(msp_uncertainty=msp_uncert_mvn, msp_fit=msp_fit, nsims=10000, subbasin_targets=mc_targets, target_weights=mc_weights) # Bonus Functions msp_smoothed <- msp_smooth(msp_sims=msp_sims_mvn) msp_pt_precip <- msp_point_precip(msp_fit=msp_fit, shp=mc_shapefile, mapping_aep=0.01, tabular_aeps=NULL) Easy right? :) B.2 Using Your Own Data from Section 2 This appendix presents an example workflow using the data you put together in Section 2. To begin, double click the RStudio Project File in the RMCmsp folder: RMCmsp.Rproj. This opens up an instance of RStudio. You should see “RMCmsp - RStudio” in the top left banner of RStudio. You will need to have devtools installed. From RStudio, type install.packages("devtools") into the Console. Once that is successfully installed, you should be able to run the following workflow: library(devtools) load_all() # Load the data anmax <- read.csv(paste0(idir, "atsite-anmax-",dur,"-day.csv")) metad <- read.csv(paste0(idir, "atsite-anmax-",dur,"-day-metadata.csv")) prism <- readRDS(paste0(idir, "prism_crop.rds")) targets0 <- read.csv(paste0(idir, "target_locations.csv")) weights <- targets0$area targets <- subset(targets0, select=c("lon","lat")) # shapefile is optional shp <- terra::vect(paste0(idir, "basin.shp")) # Step 1 msp_workspace <- msp_setup(anmax=anmax, metadata=metad, prism_rasters=prism, subbasin_targets=targets) plot_at_site_gev(at_site_gev = msp_workspace$gev.fits, shp=shp) # if you don't have shp, just remove that argument # Step 2 msp_fit <- fit_msp(msp_workspace=msp_workspace, covar.mod='twhitmat') # Step 3 msp_uncert_mvn <- msp_uncertainty(msp_fit=msp_fit, method="mvn", nsims=100) # Step 4 msp_sims_mvn <- simulate_from_msp(msp_uncertainty=msp_uncert_mvn, msp_fit=msp_fit, nsims=10000, subbasin_targets=targets, target_weights=weights) # Bonus Functions msp_smoothed <- msp_smooth(msp_sims=msp_sims_mvn) msp_pt_precip <- msp_point_precip(msp_fit=msp_fit, shp=shp, mapping_aep=0.01, tabular_aeps=NULL) Easy right? :)
